LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANTS LRICO SERVICES, LLC AND DISTILLERY NO. 209 LTD. NAPA, CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT ("FAC") IS OVERRULED AS TO ALL CAUSES OF ACTION. PLAINTIFF'S ALLEGATIONS ARE ACCEPTED AS TRUE IN RULING ON A DEMURRER. (DEL E. WEBB CORP. V. STRUCTURAL MATERIALS CO. (1981) 123 CAL. APP. 3D 593, 604.) AS TO THE FIRST CAUSE OF ACTION, PLAINTIFF HAS PLED SUFFICIENT FACTS THAT SHE WAS DISCHARGED IN VIOLATION OF LABOR CODE 216. (SEE FAC 23-24.) AS TO THE SECOND CAUSE OF ACTION, PLAINTIFF HAS PLED SUFFICIENT FACTS TO MEET THE HEIGHTENED PLEADING STANDARDS OF FRAUD, AND THAT SHE JUSTIFIABLY RELIED ON DEFENDANTS' MISREPRESENTATION THAT SHE WOULD BE AN OWNER OF THE COMPANY. (SEE FAC 9, 28-32.) AS TO THE THIRD CAUSE OF ACTION, PLAINTIFF HAS ALLEGED SUFFICIENT FACTS THAT AN ORAL PROMISE WAS MADE BY AGENTS OF DEFENDANTS THAT SHE WOULD BE AN OWNER OF THE COMPANY, AND THAT DEFENDANTS BREACHED THE ORAL PROMISE BY NOT MAKING HER AN OWNER OF THE COMPANY. (SEE FAC 9, 16.) AS TO THE FOURTH CAUSE OF ACTION, PLAINTIFF HAS ALLEGED SUFFICIENT FACTS TO SHOW THAT AGENTS OF DEFENDANTS INTENTIONALLY INTERFERED WITH THE BENEFITS OF THE ORAL PROMISE TO MAKE HER AN OWNER OF THE COMPANY. (FAC 43-45.) AS TO THE FIFTH CAUSE OF ACTION, PLAINTIFF HAS ALLEGED SUFFICIENT FACTS SHOWING DEFENDANTS MADE A PROMISE, AND THAT SHE RELIED ON TO HER DETRIMENT BY LEAVING HER PREVIOUS JOB WHICH OFFERED HIGH COMPENSATION. (SEE FAC 9, 49-50.) THE COURT DOES NOT CONSIDER GROUNDS FOR DEMURRER RAISED FOR THE FIRST TIME IN REPLY. (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER.) JUDGE: MARLA J. MILLER; CLERK: GINA GONZALES; COURT REPORTER: ANTHONY C. VAUGHN, CSR # 6185

Defendant Lrico Services, Llc, Distillery No. 209 Ltd. Napa, California Motion For Summary Judgment /Adjudication (For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
Off Calendar Jul-10-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Defendant Lrico Services, Llc, Distillery No. 209 Ltd. Napa, California Motion For Summary Judgment /Adjudication is a duplicate entry.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication
Off Calendar Jul-10-2014 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. MARCUS JACSON, TEL. NO. (760) 798-0308 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. MONICA M. QUINN OF LITTLER LAW FIRM, TEL. NO.(213) 443-4210 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.
RULING - A R G U E D;The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANTS LRICO SERVICES, LLC AND DISTILLERY NO. 209 LTD. NAPA,CA'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication -Motion for summary judgment is granted. As to the wrongful termination cause of action, Defendants met their burden showing that Plaintiff was terminated for a legitimate business reason - poor performance. (UMF nos. 7-14.) Plaintiff does not dispute these material facts. Plaintiff has not raised a triable issue of fact as to whether she was terminated because she inquired about her ownership rights. The fact that Plaintiff was terminated approximately one month after inquiring about her ownership rights is insufficient to establish a nexus. (See Loggins v. Kaiser Permanente Internat. (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 1102, 1113.) As to the breach of contract cause of action, Defendants met their burden by establishing that no oral contract was formed, as no specifics about Plaintiff's equity in the company were discussed. (UMF nos. 31-32.) Defendants also met their burden showing that Defendants did not breach any written agreement. (UMF no. 31-37.) Plaintiff has not raised a triable issue of fact as to whether Defendants breached a contract. Plaintiff concedes that the parties agreed that the specifics regarding equity "would be spelled out at a later date." (Plaintiff's response to UMF nos. 31-32.) The May 28, 2010 letter unambiguously provided that Plaintiff would be entitled to a "one-time cash bonus" if a liquidity event occurred while Plaintiff was working at the company. (Defendants' Appendix of Evidence, Ex. 4.) It is undisputed that a liquidity event never occurred while Plaintiff was employed by Defendants. (UMF no. 37.) As to the implied covenant cause of action, Plaintiff cannot establish that Defendants acted in such a way to deprive Plaintiff the benefits under any contract. (See Foley v. Interactive Data Corp. (1988) 47 Cal.3d 654, 683-84.) As discussed above, Plaintiff was not entitled to benefits because no liquidity event had occurred. As to the promissory estoppel cause of action, Plaintiff cannot establish that a clear and unambiguous promise was made. The evidence shows that no specifics about Plaintiff's equity in the company were discussed pre-hire. (UMF nos. 31-32.) As to the fraud cause of action, Defendants met their burden establishing any pre-hire discussions about equity were too vague to constitute a misrepresentation and, even if there was a misrepresentation, Plaintiff's reliance was not reasonable. (UMF nos. 27-30.) Plaintiff has not met her burden to establish a triable issue of fact. The evidence establishes that Plaintiff failed to object to the equity terms after receiving the May 18, 2010 letter. (UMF nos. 27-28.) Plaintiff understood the terms of the equity arrangement, as evidenced by her discussions about Kathryn Frederick's compensation package. (UMF no. 36.) Where an individual discovers the truth regarding an alleged misrepresentation and does not change her legal position, any alleged reliance is unreasonable. (Rochlis v. Walt Disney Co. (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 201, 215-16.) ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-16-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Caroline Burnett, Esq. of Littler Mendelson, PC, Tel. 415.399.8446, for defendant and moving party;
Marcus J. Jackson, Esq. of the Law Office of Marcus Jackson, Tel. 760.798.0308, for plaintiff and opposing party, by Cour Call.

RULING: A R G U E D. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling. DEFENDANTS LRICO SERVICES, LLC AND DISTILLERY NO. 209 LTD. NAPA, CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT ("FAC") is overruled as to all causes of action. Plaintiff's allegations are accepted as true in ruling on a demurrer. (Del E. Webb Corp. v. Structural Materials Co. (1981) 123 Cal. App. 3d 593, 604.) As to the first cause of action, Plaintiff has pled sufficient facts that she was discharged in violation of Labor Code 216. (See FAC 23-24.) As to the second cause of action, Plaintiff has pled sufficient facts to meet the heightened pleading standards of fraud, and that she justifiably relied on Defendants' misrepresentation that she would be an owner of the company. (See FAC 9, 28-32.) As to the third cause of action, Plaintiff has alleged sufficient facts that an oral promise was made by agents of Defendants that she would be an owner of the company, and that Defendants breached the oral promise by not making her an owner of the company. (See FAC 9, 16.) As to the fourth cause of action, Plaintiff has alleged sufficient facts to show that agents of Defendants intentionally interfered with the benefits of the oral promise to make her an owner of the company. (FAC 43-45.) As to the fifth cause of action, Plaintiff has alleged sufficient facts showing Defendants made a promise, and that she relied on to her detriment by leaving her previous job which offered high compensation. (See FAC 9, 49-50.) The Court does not consider grounds for demurrer raised for the first time in reply. (Prevailing party to prepare a form of order.) Judge: Marla J. Miller; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Anthony C. Vaughn, CSR # 6185
